summary: Shaw argues that the traditional art world's gatekeeping over what counts as 'art' is hypocritical, since conceptual art already stretches the definition to absurdity. He proposes an alternative definition: art is any activity that induces a state of flow requiring altered brain function. Drawing on neuroscience research about how music engages the whole brain differently, he extends this to programming, writing, painting, and dancing. He suggests flow may explain why people enjoy creative activities as a form of altered consciousness similar to meditation or religious experience. He concludes that art education should focus on teaching students to achieve flow states rather than just producing external results.
key_points: The art world's gatekeeping is hypocritical: if chicken strapped to underwear and garbage on a floor count as art, then finely crafted code qualifies too
Flow—the sensation of timelessness and effortless concentration—is the common thread connecting programming, painting, music, writing, and dancing
Neuroscience suggests creative activities repurpose brain regions for non-standard functions, which may explain both the flow state and its exhausting aftermath
Flow provides an altered consciousness similar to drugs, meditation, or religious experience but with fewer negative consequences
Art education should aim to teach students the skill of achieving flow rather than just evaluating their external output
money_quotes: Sorry nerd, you don't have neck tattoos and heroin track marks so you can't possibly be doing art.
Basically, if you want to call what you do art, then it's art. If anyone tries to tell you it's not, then they're not artists.
This intense feeling of concentration where time stops and everything in my body and mind works seamlessly as if my self doesn't exist.
It could be that flow is simply the ability to make the parts of your brain do something different for a little while. A kind of vacation for your hippocampus.

What makes coding an art is that it requires making your brain do something it's not normally designed to do which then causes a sensation similar to meditation and requires effort but feels effortless.
Instead of having to sit quietly for hours praying or meditating you can do some art, read, write, or code.
If the student is able to do this little mental trick then they'll get much more enjoyment out of the activity than just what they produce.
The best art causes this flow in others, and if you've ever seen someone play a video game or browse the internet for days on end, then you know programming beats everything in that department.
key_insight: Art should be defined not by what it produces but by the flow state it induces in the creator—a neurological altered consciousness that programming, painting, music, and writing all share.
